title: SO3 Test Protocol: Credential Lifecycle Enforcement
subtitle: Access control, non-repudiation & leakage audit

blocks.text: SO3 validates the credential lifecycle enforcement layer: the smart contracts that govern who can enroll, verify, suspend, reissue, and revoke identities on the ledger. Unlike SO1 and SO2, which are primarily quantitative, SO3 is largely deterministic: each test either passes (the contract correctly rejects the attack) or fails (the contract permits an unauthorized state transition).

The attack surface tested in SO3-T1 comprises 22 distinct vectors organized across five domains: RBAC boundary violations (e.g., a non-owner calling an owner-only function), manager delegation abuse (e.g., a delegated manager attempting to exceed their authority), device-to-node mapping violations (e.g., a registered kiosk attempting to operate on an unbound node), user suspension and revocation state machine violations (e.g., verifying against a suspended credential), and cross-contract isolation failures (e.g., one contract manipulating another's internal state).

Consent non-repudiation (SO3-T2) ensures that every lifecycle operation requires a valid EIP-712 typed-data signature from the credential holder. Three classes of signatures are tested against the contract to verify that only legitimate signatures are accepted.

blocks.caption: Signature Classes Tested (SO3-T2)
rows: Flipped s-value — tests EIP-2 low-s constraint

blocks.text: Replay and double-spend protection (SO3-T3) verifies that the per-user nonce mechanism prevents four specific attack scenarios: (1) replaying a consumed voucher after the nonce has already incremented, (2) submitting a reissuance voucher while a revocation is pending, (3) submitting a reissuance voucher against a suspended account, and (4) submitting the same voucher twice in quick succession (double-spend). Each scenario must cause the smart contract to revert with no state change.

Cross-node verification (SO3-T4) confirms that a credential enrolled on any single node in the 4-node cluster can be successfully verified from any other node. This test reuses the live cohort data from SO1-T8 but re-analyzes it per-node to confirm that FNMR and FMR do not vary significantly across kiosk locations, proving that the consensus layer correctly replicates credential state.

Template unlinkability (SO3-T5) re-evaluates the ISO 30136 D_sys metric under production conditions rather than controlled programmatic conditions (which was SO1-T4). This tests whether the unlinkability guarantee holds when templates are generated by the live deployment pipeline, including real scanner noise, environmental variance, and production cryptographic parameters.

The biometric leakage audit (SO3-T6) is a code-level inspection verifying three properties: all biometric data at rest is encrypted with AES-GCM, all data in transit is protected by devP2P (Quorum's encrypted peer-to-peer protocol) and LibP2P (IPFS's encrypted transport), and no plaintext biometric material appears anywhere on-chain, only encrypted pointers and hashes.
